title: Orange and polenta cake
subtitle: Gluten-free almond and polenta cake with orange syrup
excerpt: A dense, naturally gluten-free cake made with polenta, almond flour, and fresh orange, soaked while warm with a tart orange syrup. Improves with a day's rest. Serves eight.
sourceNotes: Polenta-based cakes using almond meal, eggs, and citrus are documented in Northern Italian domestic cooking from the nineteenth century onward. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: almond cakes made without flour using ground nuts and eggs are recorded in the Italian-style baking chapter. USDA Agricultural Research Service publications on maize (corn) document polenta's food properties and domestic baking applications; the polenta-and-almond cake combination is documented as a Lombardy regional preparation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unsoaked, cooled cake wrapped in cling film for up to 2 months. Soak and serve after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better after 24 hours. The crumb softens and the syrup distributes fully.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the polenta gives the cake a golden-orange colour that can look undercooked when it isn't — test with a skewer, which should come out clean with a few moist crumbs

content.text: Polenta gives this cake its yellow crumb and slightly grainy texture; almond flour contributes the moisture. Both are gluten-free, so there is no flour at all. The syrup poured over the warm cake is what keeps it from being dry — pour it slowly so the cake absorbs rather than sits in a puddle.

items.prepNote: about 1 tsp; use gluten-free if needed
zest and juice, about 150 ml juice

content.text: Preheat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease a 23 cm springform tin and line the base. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and creamy. Add the {{eggs}} one at a time, beating after each. Fold in the {{almond-flour}}, {{polenta}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} followed by the {{orange}} zest and juice. Pour into the prepared tin and bake for 42–47 minutes. The cake is done when it is pulling away from the sides and a skewer through the centre comes out clean or with a few moist crumbs.

While the cake bakes, bring the syrup {{caster-sugar}} and {{orange-juice}} to a simmer in a small saucepan, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Simmer for 2 minutes. Remove the cake from the oven and prick the surface all over with a skewer. Pour the warm syrup slowly over the cake while both are still warm, letting it soak in before adding more. Leave to cool completely in the tin before unmoulding.
